name: Aravinda Chakravarti
title: Muriel G & George W Singer Professor of Neuroscience & Physiology and Director, Center for Human Genetics and Genomics
summary: Contributions to Science 1. Human Genetic Variation, Non-random Recombination & Linkage Disequilibrium In the 1980’s it was close to impossible to detect linkage disequilibrium (LD) in humans since there were so few genetic markers. When identifying DNA polymorphisms using restriction fragment length polymorphisms (RFLPs) using cloned genes became possible, my laboratory was one of the first to examine rates of molecular variation using DNA markers. We detected extensive LD and pioneered its use to infer recombination patterns at the molecular level. These studies revealed many features of molecular variation and recombination, including widespread recombination hotspots. These studies were severely criticized then but have stood the test of time and presaged by a decade, waiting for the reality of the human genome sequence, the need to create human DNA sequence variation maps. a. Chakravarti A, Buetow KH, Antonarakis SE, Waber PG, Boehm CD, et al: Non-uniform recombination within the human b-globin gene cluster. Amer J Hum Genet 36:1239-1258,1984. b. Antonarakis SE, Oettgen JP, Chakravarti A, Halloran SL, Hudson RR, et al: Organization and DNA polymorphisms analysis of the human apolipoprotein AI-CIII-AIV gene cluster. Hum Genet 80:265-273,1988. c. Leitersdorf E, Chakravarti A, Hobbs HH: Polymorphic DNA haplotypes at the LDL receptor locus. Amer J Hum Genet 44:409-421,1989. d. Collins F, Guyer M, Chakravarti A: Variations on a theme: cataloging human DNA sequence variation. Science 278:1580-1581,1997. 2. Human Genetic Variation Mapping The discovery of RFLPs was a boon to complete the human meiotic map and its use for mapping Mendelian disorders. My laboratory was a leader in the construction of the human linkage map by developing statistical methods, automated computer expert systems and consensus maps. Although we mapped many Mendelian phenotypes using these marker maps we also localized genes for complex disorders. We recognized early the severe gap in identifying SNPs (single nucleotide polymorphisms) at individual genes for studies of complex disorders and performed the first large-scale coding (hypertension) gene-based SNP study in 1999. With the completion of the human genome draft sequence in 2000, I championed with David Altshuler and Mark Daly the comprehensive study of SNPs and their LD patterns across the human genome in 2002. These landmark studies, the HapMap and the 1000 Genomes project, to which I contributed design, analysis and leadership, have had a seismic and unimagined effect in human genetics. a. NIH/CEPH Collaborative Mapping Group: A comprehensive genetic linkage map of the human genome. Science 258:67-86,1992. b. Halushka MK, Fan J-B, Bentley K, Hsie L, Weder A, Cooper R, Lipshutz R, Chakravarti A: Patterns of single nucleotide polymorphisms in candidate genes regulating blood pressure homeostasis. Nat Genet 22:239-247,1999. c. The International HapMap Consortium: A second-generation human haplotype map of over 3.1 million SNPs. Nature 449:851-861,2007. d. The 1000 Genomes Project Consortium: A global reference for human genome variation. Nature 526:68-74, 2015. 3. Genome-wide Association Studies (GWAS) The identification of CFTR was a landmark discovery because it was accomplished without clues from a local chromosomal change. Rather, CFTR and its major CF mutation were identified using LD mapping by my group in collaboration with Lap-Chee Tsui at Toronto. My earlier work with b-globin suggested that a putative common CF mutation could be identified this way. This led Tsui to develop a high-resolution marker map in the CF linkage interval which we analyzed for LD patterns and predicted one common and several rarer mutant alleles (by haplotypes) and the probable location of the common mutant, eventually leading to identification of DF508. This success in individuals of European ancestry suggested a broader application of LD mapping but required an estimated marker resolution of 20 kb or better (then

name: Molly Gale Hammell
title: Associate Professor
summary:
Molly Gale Hammell is an Associate Professor in the Institute for Systems Genetics at NYU Langone Health. Her research portfolio centers on computational and quantitative genetic approaches, focusing on the regulation and function of transposable elements in the human genome, RNA biology, and data-driven studies of neurodegeneration, particularly Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). She is recognized for her contributions to developing novel statistical and algorithmic tools for genomic data analysis and for uncovering mechanisms that drive disease processes at the genetic and molecular level. Her interdisciplinary expertise spans genetics, genomics, systems biology, and human disease, positioning her at the forefront of research that connects mechanistic insights to translational and clinical relevance. More recently, her lab has been exploring the ability of transposable elements derived RNA and DNA species to activate innate immune pathways, driving neuroinflammation.

Molly Gale Hammell is an Associate Professor in the Institute for Systems Genetics at NYU Langone Health. Her research portfolio centers on computational and quantitative genetic approaches, focusing on the regulation and function of transposable elements in the human genome, RNA biology, and data-driven studies of neurodegeneration, particularly Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). She is recognized for her contributions to developing novel statistical and algorithmic tools for genomic data analysis and for uncovering mechanisms that drive disease processes at the genetic and molecular level. Her interdisciplinary expertise spans genetics, genomics, systems biology, and human disease, positioning her at the forefront of research that connects mechanistic insights to translational and clinical relevance. Current interests include neuroinflammatory triggers driven by transposable elements and innate immune pathways
